sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, LEFT, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 60, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: RIGHT and LEFT both blocked! Player stuck at cols 25-27. G frame at cols 20-23 blocks LEFT. Need to go around.
- Goal model: Collect all three blue-square targets by moving adjacent and pressing SPACE.
- Action model: Movement moves player 4 cells. SPACE collects adjacent targets.
- Plan: Move UP to rows 28-31, then LEFT to cols 21-23, then DOWN to rows 32-35 to get adjacent to target at cols 16-19.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 59 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Good, the player moved! UP (44 cells), LEFT (33 cells), DOWN (18 cells) all changed the board. Let me check the current state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

print("Current nodes:")
for n in nodes:
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Player position
green = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'N']
white = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'W']
print(f"\nGreen: {green[0]['boundary'] if green else 'NOT FOUND'}")
print(f"White: {white[0]['boundary'] if white else 'NOT FOUND'}")

# Target frames
c_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 12]
print(f"\nTarget frames (c, 12px): {len(c_nodes)}")
for cn in c_nodes:
    print(f"  boundary={cn['boundary']}")

# Check for G frames
G_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'G' and n['pixels'] == 12]
print(f"G frames: {len(G_nodes)}")
for gn in G_nodes:
    print(f"  boundary={gn['boundary']}")

# Blue centers
b_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 4]
print(f"Blue centers (4px): {len(b_nodes)}")
for bn in b_nodes:
    print(f"  boundary={bn['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
Now I see the state:
- Green: rows 28-30, cols 20-23 (moved UP and LEFT, now at rows 28-30 instead of 32-35)
- White: rows 31, cols 20-23 (below green)
- G frame: rows 32-35, cols 20-23 (below white)
- Target at rows 32-35, cols 16-19 (to the LEFT of G frame)
- Target at rows 20-23, cols 40-43 (top right, still uncollected)
So the green block moved UP to rows 28-30, then LEFT to cols 20-23, then DOWN back to... wait, the green is at rows 28-30, not rows 32-35. So the DOWN didn't fully move it back down.
Actually wait, looking more carefully:
- Green: [[28, 20], [28, 23], [30, 23], [30, 20]] - this is rows 28-30, cols 20-23
- White: [[31, 20], [31, 23]] - this is row 31, cols 20-23
- G frame: [[32, 20], [32, 23], [35, 23], [35, 20]] - this is rows 32-35, cols 20-23
So the green moved UP (from rows 32-35 to rows 28-31), then LEFT (from cols 25-27 to cols 21-23... but it's at cols 20-23 now), then DOWN (from rows 28-31 to rows 29-32... but it's at rows 28-30).
Hmm, the player is now at rows 28-30, cols 20-23. The white is at row 31, cols 20-23. The G frame is at rows 32-35, cols 20-23. The target is at rows 32-35, cols 16-19.
The green block is now ABOVE the G frame, not to the RIGHT of it. The white block is between the green and the G frame.
Now I need to get to the target at cols 16-19. The green is at cols 20-23. If I move LEFT, the green would go to cols 16-19, which is where the target is! But the target is at rows 32-35, and the green is at rows 28-30. So moving LEFT would put the green at rows 28-30, cols 16-19, which doesn't overlap with the target at rows 32-35, cols 16-19.
I need to move DOWN to get the green to rows 32-35, then LEFT to cols 16-19. But the G frame is at rows 32-35, cols 20-23. If I move DOWN, the green would go to rows 32-35, cols 20-23, which overlaps with the G frame.
Wait, but the green is at rows 28-30 and the G frame is at rows 32-35. There's a gap of row 31 (which is the white block). If I move DOWN, the green would go to rows 32-35, cols 20-23, which overlaps with the G frame. This would be like moving INTO the G frame, which might activate it or collect it.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The target at rows 32-35, cols 16-19 needs to be activated. The activation happens when the white block is adjacent to the target. Currently:
- White is at row 31, cols 20-23
- Target is at rows 32-35, cols 16-19
- They're not adjacent (white is above and to the right of the target)
If I move DOWN, the green goes to rows 32-35, cols 20-23 (overlapping G frame), and the white goes to row 31, cols 20-23 (stays or moves?). Actually, the white block seems to be the row BELOW the green block. So if green moves DOWN to rows 32-35, the white would be at rows 28-30 (the green's previous position)?
No wait, looking at the current state:
- Green: rows 28-30, cols 20-23 (3 rows x 4 cols = 12 pixels)
- White: row 31, cols 20-23 (1 row x 4 cols = 4 pixels)
The white is 1 row x 4 cols, which is different from before when it was 4 rows x 1 col. This is because the player moved UP (vertical movement), so the white trail is horizontal (1 row wide).
Before, when the player moved LEFT (horizontal movement), the white trail was vertical (1 col wide, 4 rows tall).
So the white trail's orientation depends on the last movement direction!
Now, if I move DOWN, the green would go to rows 32-35, cols 20-23 (overlapping G frame), and the white would be at rows 31, cols 20-23 (the row between green's old and new position).
Actually, I think the white block is always at the "edge" of the green block in the direction opposite to the last movement. When the player moves DOWN, the white block is ABOVE the green block. When the player moves UP, the white block is BELOW the green block. When the player moves LEFT, the white block is to the RIGHT of the green block. When the player moves RIGHT, the white block is to the LEFT of the green block.
Wait, that doesn't match. Let me re-examine:
- After moving UP: green at rows 28-30, white at row 31 (below green) - white is in the direction of movement
- After moving LEFT: green at cols 20-23, white at... hmm, I need to check the intermediate states.
Actually, let me just try moving DOWN and see what happens. The green is at rows 28-30, cols 20-23. Moving DOWN would put the green at rows 32-35, cols 20-23, which overlaps with the G frame. This might collect the G frame or activate the target.
